pageDescription: What is InlineXML and how did it come to be.
content: [{"id":"38d08f89-1351-41f3-ab9d-67e831a24429","component":"Text","data":{"content":"UI frameworks in C# tend to follow an imperative model. Even frameworks that use XML-based layouts still rely heavily on bindings and additional framework abstractions. While these systems describe structure, they often introduce significant overhead and still require imperative logic to coordinate state and UI updates.","tag":"p","align":"left","weight":"regular","color":"","fontSize":"","fontFamily":"Inter, sans-serif","lineHeight":"","letterSpacing":"","marginBottom":"","className":""},"children":[]},{"id":"bc88d04d-ee0a-4940-9ac4-a4483f896023","component":"Text","data":{"content":"In contrast, JSX takes a declarative approach. Instead of describing how the UI should be updated step by step, JSX expresses what the UI should look like for a given state. The rendering system then determines how to efficiently update the DOM to match that description.","tag":"p","align":"left","weight":"regular","color":"","fontSize":"","fontFamily":"Inter, sans-serif","lineHeight":"","letterSpacing":"","marginBottom":"","className":""},"children":[]},{"id":"7e7739c7-c7a7-4921-af49-170101760faf","component":"Text","data":{"content":"Moving from JSX to building UIs in C# highlights a significant difference in developer experience. JSX focuses on describing structure and intent, allowing the framework to handle reconciliation and rendering. This separation between state and rendering logic makes UI composition more predictable and easier to reason about.","tag":"p","align":"left","weight":"regular","color":"","fontSize":"","fontFamily":"Inter, sans-serif","lineHeight":"","letterSpacing":"","marginBottom":"","className":""},"children":[]},{"id":"902de4ba-ba21-4b53-b38e-c9f38a217fe0","component":"Text","data":{"content":"This led me to an interesting question. What if it were possible to write a JSX-like syntax in C# to describe UI structure? If C# could express UI declaratively in a way similar to JSX, it could significantly improve how UIs are authored in .NET.","tag":"p","align":"left","weight":"regular","color":"","fontSize":"","fontFamily":"Inter, sans-serif","lineHeight":"","letterSpacing":"","marginBottom":"","className":""},"children":[]},{"id":"1afa0dcf-b1cc-46a3-9b3c-e5035296d010","component":"Text","data":{"content":"This becomes even more interesting when considering the future of WebAssembly. As WASM support continues to mature, it raises the possibility of building web applications entirely with .NET, potentially removing the need for JavaScript in many cases.","tag":"p","align":"left","weight":"regular","color":"","fontSize":"","fontFamily":"Inter, sans-serif","lineHeight":"","letterSpacing":"","marginBottom":"","className":""},"children":[]},{"id":"85862587-9b68-4138-9a35-fd9a5e3c6cbf","component":"Text","data":{"content":"To explore this idea, I started experimenting with a new concept called InlineXML. The goal was to allow developers to write markup-like structures directly inside C# code, enabling a declarative way to describe UI components while still benefiting from the .NET ecosystem and type system.","tag":"p","align":"left","weight":"regular","color":"","fontSize":"","fontFamily":"Inter, sans-serif","lineHeight":"","letterSpacing":"","marginBottom":"","className":""},"children":[]}]
